quality.rationale: 结合相对论伯努利方程、热力学第一定律与声速定义的多步代数推导，具有明确的喷流/风洞物理背景且解答完整

origin.question: Problem 5.25. A relativistic wind tunnel is to be fed from a tank of perfect adiabatic compressed gas. Suppose the gas has an equation of state $p \propto n^{\gamma}$ with $\gamma$ constant, and the speed of sound in the tank is $a$ . What is the largest wind velocity $v_{\max}$ which can be obtained? (No gravitational forces; isentropic flow.)
origin.solution: Solution 5.25. The relativistic Bernoulli equation, which expresses the conservation of energy along flow lines of a perfect fluid, is (Problem 14.7)$$( 1 - v^{2} )^{- \frac {1} {2}} = \left( \frac {n} {P + \rho}\right) \times c o n s t a n t .$$(This equation follows from $\mathrm{T}^{0\nu}, _{\nu} = 0$ , from $(\mathrm{nu}^{\mu})_{,\mu} = 0$ and from the first law of thermodynamics $\mathrm{d}\rho / (\mathrm{P} + \rho) = \mathrm{dn} / \mathrm{n}$ .) Since the gas starts in the tank at zero velocity, we have: constant $= (\mathrm{P}_0 + \rho_0) / \mathrm{n}_0$ . An upper bound for $\mathfrak{n} / (\mathbb{P} + \rho)$ which will give an upper bound $\mathbf{v}_{\max}$ for $\mathbf{v}$ , occurs when $\mathrm{P} \to 0$ implying $\rho = \mathfrak{mn}$ , so$$\left( 1 - \mathrm {v}_{\mathrm {m a x}}^{2}\right)^{- \frac {1} {2}} = \frac {1} {\mathrm {m}} \left( \frac {\mathrm {P}_{0} + \rho_{0}} {\mathrm {n}_{0}}\right) .$$Now we have to try and write the right hand side only in terms of $\mathbf{a}$ , the sound velocity of the gas with $\mathbf{P}_0, \rho_0, \mathbf{n}_0$ . We have $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{Kn}^{\gamma}$ where $\mathbf{K}$ is a constant telling what adiabat the gas is on. The first law reads$$\frac {\mathrm {d} \rho} {\mathrm {d} n} = \frac {\mathrm {K n}^{\gamma} + \rho} {n}$$which is integrable and (with $\rho \to \mathfrak{mn}$ when $\mathfrak{n} \to 0$ ) gives$$\rho = \mathrm {m n} + \frac {\mathrm {K}} {\gamma - 1} \mathrm {n}^{\gamma} .$$The speed of sound a is given by$$a^{2} = \frac {d P} {d \rho} = \frac {d P / d n} {d \rho / d n} = \frac {\gamma K n^{\gamma - 1}} {m + \gamma K n^{\gamma - 1} / ( \gamma - 1 )} \tag {1}$$and the first law of thermodynamics gives us$$\frac {\mathrm {P} + \rho} {\mathrm {n}} = \frac {\mathrm {d} \rho} {\mathrm {d n}} = \left( \frac {\mathrm {d P}} {\mathrm {d n}}\right) \left( \frac {\mathrm {d} \rho} {\mathrm {d P}}\right) = ( \gamma \mathrm {K n}^{\gamma - 1} ) ( 1 / \mathrm {a}^{2} ) . \tag {2}$$But Equation (1) contains just this same combination $\gamma \mathrm{Kn}^{\gamma - 1}$ . So we can solve Equation (1) for$$\gamma \mathrm {K n}^{\gamma - 1} = \mathrm {m a}^{2} / [ 1 - \mathrm {a}^{2} / ( \gamma - 1 ) ]$$and get$$\frac {1} {m} \left( \frac {P_{0} + \rho_{0}} {n_{0}}\right) = \frac {1} {1 - a^{2} / ( \gamma - 1 )}$$and therefore$$\mathbf {v}_{\mathrm {m a x}}^{2} = \mathbf {1} - [ 1 - \mathbf {a}^{2} / ( \gamma - 1 ) ]^{2} .$$(Notice that $\mathbf{v}_{\max} \rightarrow 1$ in the limit of highly relativistic gas, for which $\gamma \rightarrow 4/3$ and $a^2 \rightarrow 1/3$ .)
